headlines.geopolitics: Community honors 'unclaimed' Navy veteran with full military funeral
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting billion-dollar program abuse
headlines.domestic: Senate advances sweeping housing bill ahead of midterm elections

metadata.daily_do: Check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran you know — a simple conversation can make all the difference

clips.caption: When Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Duke's take on government fraud is... intense
The potato harassment case that broke Jax

script.text: The Romans had a saying — dulce et decorum est pro patria mori — it is sweet and proper to die for one's country. But what they never said was what happens when you die for your country and no one comes to claim you.

Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th, 2026. Let's start with what happened over the weekend. In geopolitics — a Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman was buried with full military honors at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery after being classified as 'unclaimed' with no known family.
Oh wow.

Community members and veterans filled the service to make sure he wasn't buried alone. The VA representative said quote, 'unclaimed' was incorrect — that community support proved every veteran can be claimed.
Good for them.
In economy — Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division to combat what he called an 'insane' problem of Medicaid and federal program fraud.
How insane are we talking?

Two Pennsylvania men allegedly received four million dollars in federal funds for homeless shelters in Minneapolis that didn't exist. The new division promises prosecution for any fraud over one thousand dollars.
A thousand dollars? That's... aggressive.
About time.

And domestically — the Senate is moving toward passing comprehensive housing legislation addressing affordability issues that are expected to dominate the midterm elections. Progress is notable given how contentious housing policy typically is.
Finally.
Wait, Congress is actually doing something about housing? What timeline are we in?
We'll get to all of that. But first — let's talk about Lonnie Wayman.

So here's what happened. Lonnie Wayman was a Navy veteran. When he died, no family came forward to claim his remains. The paperwork marked him as 'unclaimed.' But when the community heard about it, veterans groups and regular people showed up to his funeral. Packed the place.
How does someone just... have no family? I mean, that's heartbreaking.
Happens more than you think. Military life can be isolating. Deployments, moves, relationships that don't survive the distance.
But like, the community just... showed up? That's actually beautiful.

The VA representative made a point at the service. He said the word 'unclaimed' was wrong because the community claimed him. Quote — 'We are able to claim our honorable veterans and provide them the dignity and honor that they have earned.'
That's— okay, that actually made me tear up a little. But it also raises questions about the system, right? Like, how many other veterans are sitting in morgues right now marked as 'unclaimed'?
The system works when people make it work. You can't bureaucratize caring. These folks in Tennessee did what neighbors used to do automatically.
But Duke, that's exactly my point. We shouldn't have to rely on random acts of kindness for basic dignity. The VA should have better systems for—
Better systems for what? Forcing people to care? You can't mandate community, Bree.
I'm not talking about mandating community. I'm talking about better family tracing, better record-keeping, proactive outreach before someone dies alone and—

Can I just say something? This story hit me because like, my dad drives the bus in Revere, right? And he knows every regular passenger. Mrs. Chen who rides to dialysis on Tuesdays, the guy who works the night shift at the hospital. When Mrs. Chen didn't show up for three weeks, my dad called the route supervisor to check on her.
What happened to Mrs. Chen?
She was fine, just switched to a different dialysis center. But the point is, my dad noticed. Someone noticed. And these people in Tennessee noticed Lonnie Wayman.
Exactly. That's what I'm talking about. Your dad didn't need a federal program to tell him to care about Mrs. Chen.
And that's wonderful, but what about the places where there is no Mrs. Chen? What about the veterans who don't have a community like Tennessee? The data shows—
Hold on, Bree. What data?
VA records show thousands of unclaimed remains annually. Not all of them get the Tennessee treatment. Most get buried in unmarked graves or cremated and stored in funeral homes indefinitely.
So what's your solution? A federal Department of Making Sure People Have Friends?
Better veteran services integration. Proactive wellness checks. Community liaison programs. It's not rocket science, Duke.
And it's also not the federal government's job to be everyone's best friend. Sometimes people are alone because they choose to be alone.
Okay, but can we agree that what happened in Tennessee was good? Like, regardless of policy debates, a bunch of people showed up for a stranger's funeral because it was the right thing to do?
I think we can all agree on that. The question is whether it should be the exception or the rule.
It should be the rule. And that requires systems, not just hoping for the kindness of strangers.
It is the rule in most places. Most communities do take care of their own. We just don't hear about it because it's not news when things work right.
Let me bring in Professor Pringle on this. Doc, you've been quiet. This has to remind you of something historical.
Indeed it does, Leo. The story of Lonnie Wayman takes me back to November 11th, 1921. The dedication of the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier at Arlington National Cemetery.
Oh wow, we're going back to 1921. I'm buckling in.

After the Great War, there were thousands of unidentified American dead. Bodies so damaged by artillery and gas that they could not be identified. The Army selected four unknowns from different battlefields in France.
This is already devastating.

Sergeant Edward Younger, himself a veteran, was chosen to select one casket. He walked around each casket several times, then placed a bouquet of white roses on one. That soldier became the Unknown Soldier.
And we've been guarding that tomb ever since.
Twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, since 1937. Through hurricanes, blizzards, the attacks of September 11th. The Old Guard maintains their vigil.
What's the parallel you're drawing, Professor?

The Unknown Soldier represents every unidentified service member. But Lonnie Wayman was not unknown — he was unclaimed. The community in Tennessee performed the same function as Sergeant Younger. They claimed him.
Oh damn. That's actually beautiful.

Throughout history, societies have grappled with how to honor military dead who have no one to mourn them. The Romans created the cult of the war dead. The British built the Cenotaph. We created Arlington.
But those are all institutional responses, Doc. Government responses.

Quite right, Bree. But they began as community responses. Families placing flowers on graves. Neighbors maintaining monuments. The institutions came later to formalize what communities were already doing.
See? Community first, government second.

The question, Duke, is what happens when community fails. During the Civil War, Clara Barton spent decades searching for missing soldiers because families couldn't do it alone. Sometimes institutions must step in where community cannot reach.
Thank you. That's exactly what I'm saying.

The inscription on the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier reads 'Here rests in honored glory an American soldier known but to God.' Lonnie Wayman was known to God — and thanks to Tennessee, known to us as well.
Thank you, Professor. That's a beautiful way to frame it.
I need a minute. That was heavier than I expected for a Monday morning.
Let's shift gears. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division last week, and the numbers he cited are staggering.
Finally. Do you know how much money we're losing to fraud every year?
Blanche gave one example — two men from Pennsylvania who got four million dollars in federal funding for homeless shelters in Minneapolis. The shelters didn't exist.
Wait, they just... asked for money for fake homeless shelters and got it?
They got signatures from actual homeless people in Minneapolis and promised they'd receive appropriate care. Then took the money and ran.
That's horrific. Those are real people who needed help and these guys used them as props for fraud.
And this is why I don't trust big government programs. There's no accountability. Blanche said the feds give money away like candy.
The problem isn't the programs, Duke. The problem is inadequate oversight. These programs help millions of people legitimately.
And they also help millions of people fraudulently, apparently. School lunch programs, daycare centers that don't exist — how do you not notice that a daycare center doesn't exist?
The new division promises prosecution for any fraud over a thousand dollars. Quote — 'If you steal $1001, that's a felony, and you are going to go to prison.'
A thousand bucks? That seems low for federal prosecution.
Low? It's perfect. You want to stop fraud? Make it hurt. Make people believe they'll actually get caught and go to prison for stealing taxpayer money.
But is that cost-effective? Federal prosecutors going after thousand-dollar fraud cases? The court costs alone—
Bree, you're missing the point. It's about deterrence. If people think they can steal a thousand bucks and nothing happens, they'll steal ten thousand next time.
I get deterrence, but there's also something called prosecutorial discretion. Should federal resources really go toward—
Yes. Absolutely yes. Every dollar stolen from taxpayers is a dollar that doesn't go to people who actually need it.
Okay, but like, are we sure the system is ready for this? Because if they're prosecuting everyone who steals a thousand bucks, the courts are gonna be backed up until 2030.
That's actually a fair point. The Justice Department is creating an entirely new division with a dedicated assistant attorney general, which suggests they're expecting a lot of cases.
And that means a lot of fraud is happening. Which brings me back to my original point — maybe we should focus on preventing fraud instead of just prosecuting it after the fact.
You can't prevent fraud, Bree. People are gonna try to steal money. The only thing that stops them is knowing they'll get caught and punished.
But you can make it harder. Better verification systems, cross-referencing databases, requiring physical inspections—
More bureaucracy. More delays. More barriers for legitimate people who need help.
It's not more bureaucracy, it's competent administration. There's a difference.
Alright, let's move on. We've got housing legislation making unexpected progress in the Senate.
Oh right, the thing I joked about earlier. Congress actually doing something.
The Times reports that comprehensive housing legislation is moving forward, addressing affordability issues that are expected to dominate the midterms.
This is huge. Housing costs are the number one economic issue for most Americans right now.
And it's also a local issue, not a federal one. Zoning is handled by cities and counties, not Washington.
But federal policy can incentivize local reform. Tax credits for cities that eliminate single-family zoning, funding tied to housing production targets—
So bribery. The federal government bribing local governments to ignore their voters' preferences on zoning.
It's not bribery, it's policy alignment. And voters' preferences often reflect NIMBY attitudes that make housing unaffordable for everyone else.
NIMBY?
Not In My Backyard. People who want affordable housing in theory but fight against it in their neighborhood.
Or people who bought homes in neighborhoods they liked and don't want them turned into apartment complexes. That's not NIMBY, that's property rights.
We're going to have to leave that there. Time for Rapid Fire with Jax.

Alright everybody, time to cleanse the palate with some truly bizarre news. First up — an Irish employee is suing his boss for workplace harassment because the boss repeatedly shouted 'potato' at him.
Potato?
Potato. Like, just the word 'potato.' Apparently this went on for months. The guy would walk by and his boss would just yell 'POTATO!' at him.
That's... that's actually really messed up. Irish potato famine references? That's not funny, that's ethnic harassment.
Right? Like, if someone kept yelling 'pasta' at me because I'm Italian, I'd be pissed too. Except I'm not Italian, I'm Irish, so I guess they'd yell 'potato' at me too.
Wait, you're Irish?
Mooney? From Revere? Come on, Duke. Next story — Palantir's AI weapons technology has been reassigned to help the government find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?
The same AI that was designed to identify enemy combatants is now being used to locate ergonomic office furniture for federal employees. Apparently there's a chair shortage.
This reminds me of the Pentagon's infamous toilet seat scandal of the 1980s.
Doc, please tell me the government didn't spend millions on toilet seats.
Six hundred dollars per toilet seat for the C-5A Galaxy transport aircraft. The chairs, I suspect, will cost considerably more.
Of course they will. Next — Louisiana schools must display the Ten Commandments in classrooms by Friday. This is apparently still happening.
Oh, the separation of church and state lawsuit is gonna be massive.
Good. Maybe kids will learn something about moral behavior for once.
Duke, public schools can't—
Nope, nope, we're moving on. Last story — a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt.
Months?
Months. The guy survived on gas station snacks and energy drinks. When they finally found him, he'd organized the entire storage unit and written a memoir on the back of cardboard boxes.
Florida Man strikes again.
That's kidnapping. That's literally kidnapping.
It's Florida. Different rules apply. The memoir is apparently being shopped to publishers as we speak.
The Count of Monte Cristo meets Breaking Bad. Alexandre Dumas would be fascinated.
And that's your Rapid Fire for today. Leo, take us home.
Time for the Daily Do. Today's story about Lonnie Wayman reminded me that there are probably veterans in your community right now who could use some company.

So here's what you can do today — check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran you know. Not because the government told you to, not because of any program or policy. Just because it's the right thing to do.

A simple conversation can make all the difference. Ask how they're doing. Listen to their stories. Maybe they'll tell you about their service, maybe they won't. Either way, they'll know someone cares.
That's actually perfect, Leo.
Community in action.
Professor, take us out with a quote.
From the inscription at Arlington National Cemetery — 'Here rests in honored glory an American soldier known but to God.' Today, let's make sure fewer soldiers rest known only to God.
Damn, Doc. You always nail the landing.
I'm Leo Barros. Thanks for listening to The Friction. We'll see you tomorrow.
